questions.question: A casting shows a ragged, branched crack exactly where a thick boss joins a thin wall. What is the most likely discontinuity?
options: Cold shut
Hot tear
Gas porosity
Seam
questions.explanation: Ragged, branched cracking at a section-thickness change is the hot tear signature — the mold restrained the casting while it was still hot and weak. A cold shut would be smooth and rounded.

questions.question: The first machining cut on a casting exposes a cluster of smooth, shiny-walled, round cavities. Which discontinuity does this appearance indicate?
options: Shrinkage — jagged and dendritic
Sand inclusion with embedded mold material
Gas porosity — rounded holes left by dissolved gas coming out of solution
Hot tear at a section change
questions.explanation: Smooth, rounded, shiny-walled cavities were shaped by gas, not by tearing. Shrinkage looks torn and spongy, and inclusions contain foreign material.

questions.question: Which statement correctly explains why laminations exist in rolled plate?
options: Ingot pipe, porosity, and inclusions were flattened parallel to the plate surfaces during rolling
Weld spatter was rolled into the plate at the mill
Corrosion separated the plate layers in service
Excess weld reinforcement was ground flush, leaving a plane of weakness
questions.explanation: Laminations are inherent ingot discontinuities — pipe, porosity, inclusions — flattened into planar separations by the rolling reduction, which is why they lie parallel to the plate surfaces.

questions.question: On a fatigue fracture face, the final-fracture zone is small compared with the smooth, beach-marked area. What does that proportion tell you?
options: The part failed on the very first load cycle
The fracture is not fatigue at all
The material was defective when it left the mill
The running stress was relatively low and the crack grew through many cycles before final overload
questions.explanation: The final zone is the ligament that tore in one overload event. A small final zone means little section was needed to carry the modest running load, so the crack had a long propagation life.

questions.question: Which location is the classic home of crevice corrosion?
options: Open, frequently washed surfaces at mid-plate
The outer radius of a piping elbow
Under gaskets, washers, lap joints, and deposits where oxygen is starved
The suction face of a pump impeller
questions.explanation: Crevice corrosion needs an oxygen-starved gap — gaskets, bolt heads, lap joints, and dried deposits create exactly that chemistry. The elbow radius is erosion territory; the impeller face is cavitation territory.

questions.question: A pump impeller shows clusters of jagged, spongy pits concentrated on the low-pressure face. Identify the mechanism.
options: Erosion — smooth, scalloped thinning
Uniform corrosion
Fretting
Cavitation — collapsing vapor bubbles hammering the surface
questions.explanation: Spongy, honeycomb-like pit clusters on low-pressure impeller faces are the cavitation signature. Erosion polishes and scallops smoothly; fretting lives in clamped joints, not flow paths.

questions.question: A relevant indication is evaluated against the acceptance criteria and found acceptable. Which statement is correct?
options: It is a defect, just a small one
The discontinuity may remain in the part; it is recorded and accepted — defect applies only to rejectable discontinuities
It must still be removed before the part ships
It is reclassified as a nonrelevant indication
questions.explanation: Defect is reserved for discontinuities that violate the criteria. An evaluated, acceptable discontinuity stays a discontinuity — documented, accepted, and legitimate in the part.

questions.question: A procedure states that weld surface texture shall be at least as good as workmanship sample number three. Evaluating to this criterion is an example of:
options: Size-based measurement
A verbal description criterion
Grading by comparison with a standard
Visual-tactile recognition alone
questions.explanation: A workmanship sample is a comparison standard: the technique is side-by-side viewing under matched lighting, assigning the nearest grade, and recording the reference identity.

questions.question: A sketch in an image report is most useful when it includes:
options: A datum reference, indication IDs matching the data report, key dimensions, and a not-to-scale note where applicable
Shading and perspective so it looks realistic
Only the part outline, to avoid clutter
A color code known only to the examiner who drew it
questions.explanation: A sketch is evidence only if a stranger can use it: datum, orientation, cross-referenced indication IDs, and honest scale information make it reproducible.

questions.question: During the final completion-checklist review you find indication VT-12 in the log with no disposition recorded. What does this demonstrate about the checklist?
options: That it is bureaucratic overhead with no field value
That it catches open items — testing is not complete until every indication is dispositioned and the record closed
That the examiner must be decertified immediately
That the entire examination must always be re-performed from scratch
questions.explanation: This is exactly why CP-105 requires completion to be confirmed with a checklist: it catches orphan indications and record gaps before the test is declared finished, while they can still be fixed.
